data.title: Cardinal Energy: The Risk Rises (Rating And Outlook Downgrade)
data.description:
The last article on Cardinal Energy (CRLFF) covered the secondary recovery and the venture into the thermal business. Neither of these businesses are considered low-cost businesses. Additionally, this is an upstream company with a dividend model that it has made work so far. Nonetheless, the current political uncertainty would argue for heading towards larger and more integrated companies to get past any coming industry challenges. Currently undervalued Cenovus Energy (CVE) would be one of several Canadian ideas that I write about. This is not the kind of company to be invested in when there are threats of trade wars and tariffs while Canada is bargaining with the United States over a new trade agreement.

Stock Price

As an income idea, this stock has long traded in a range. Since the last article the stock has appreciated nicely for an income idea to trade near the top of its trading range.

The biggest change going forward is the end of the thermal construction project that has marked a use of cash flow and debt accumulation for some time. Therefore, as the thermal project begins production in the near future, there will be a switch from the negative cash flow required for the capital phase of the project to the project becoming cash flow positive.

That could potentially provide a temporary boost to the stock price. Whether it is enough to justify continuing to hold the stock a bit longer is an open question.

Earnings

As shown below, there has been some concern about the dividend payout ratio. However, that payout ratio is subject to capital project plans. As noted before, those plans are now proceeding to the production phase of the thermal project.

(Cardinal Energy is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

The same press release noted that the thermal project is now proceeding towards production. That will not only add to the volumes produced reported, but it will also increase cash flow in the near future.

The debt ratio is still satisfactory as it is less than the top limit of 1.5. However, commodity prices are weak, and the United States and Canada are bargaining over trade agreements. Usually, the United States is the biggest customer for heavy oil and thermal oil production. So, in the short-term there could be some uncertainty about sales and pricing if that bargaining is not replaced with something that is both constructive and permanent.

Increasing Risk

The increasing risk comes from both of the ideas that secondary recovery is not the lowest cost way to produce (that belongs to the unconventional and conventional producers) combined with the idea that this management wants to expand to more thermal projects.

The secondary recovery projects have been long-term successes because Canada has low costs in the traditional upstream business. Therefore, higher costs for secondary recovery in Canada often still offer competitive costs when compared to the United States business.

That has allowed this company to get considerable mileage out of its dividend model. That is especially true when it was noted that the debt ratio was unusually low. That is likewise considered a necessity for lower cost producers.

Now the debt ratio has risen as the thermal project proceeded with the capital investment phase necessary before startup. But now, production should soon begin to climb on this project to provide cash flow instead of using cash flow.

The issue with the risk arises from the fact that thermal projects, especially new ones, are seldom low cost. Oftentimes, it takes a few add-on projects to lower the cost. On the other hand, the large capital needed upfront to begin one of these projects often assures a decent cash flow even when the project loses money.

The uncertainty caused by threats of trade wars and tariffs could well mean that this project will lose money first before making money. A successful expansion would appear to depend upon the two countries having a constructive and permanent replacement to the current bargaining. But that could take some time.

Instead, there is every possibility that the current political uncertainty could bring on a North American recession marked by weak commodity prices. That could more than cancel out the benefits of additional production to result in at least temporarily reduced cash flow.

When that uncertainty is combined with the recent debt ratio, things could become more challenging than this company is used to.

Specifically, the price of oil is currently below the assumed price of WTI $65. If anything, that oil price is threatening to go lower.

Secondly, the assumed discount often widens during times of weak oil prices. That is bad news for a project that is just starting up after piling up a lot of debt to get started in the first place.

Long-term, the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline has kept the discount in check. But it has also caused a rash of projects like this one to start because the profitability of thermal has improved with the startup of the pipeline.

But all those new projects (including this one) threaten to bring back the old conditions. Now admittedly there is talk of still another pipeline to expand export capacity in Canada. But that pipeline has got to move past the talking phase to the actual existence and used phase to be of any help to the long-term outlook here. Good luck with that happening any time soon.

Summary

Cardinal Energy had an income model that was working because it was low debt and the secondary recovery was reasonably profitable. But that model has now materially changed with a much higher (although still acceptable) debt ratio and the entrance into the very volatile thermal business.

For that reason, this stock moves to a hold with a negative outlook until the future clears up somewhat. Personally, I would prefer to be elsewhere even though the track record here of the income model is so far impressive.

Investing is about avoiding risk. Therefore, the need to guess as to whether or not this model succeeds through a potential economic downturn is really more about accepting the risk of guessing correctly. I personally choose to avoid that risk even though others may not worry about it.

There is a lot to like about the company. But not in the current environment.

Risks

Canada and the United States could have a constructive and permanent agreement tomorrow that would allow for a re-evaluation of the situation in a positive direction.

The United States also has issues in that it is attempting to stimulate the economy by borrowing more money when there is already a high debt load. Should the United States reach the end of its borrowing ability at reasonable rates, there could be a reckoning that will not be pleasant for either country.

Even with the increasing deficit and low interest rates, United States consumers are far from happy. Meanwhile the Dallas Federal Reserve Reported upstream industry shrinkage in the third quarter survey and a variety of comments depending upon producer size in the fourth quarter survey. The shrinkage continued in the fourth quarter. This will affect oil supply and hence imports needed from Canada if this continues. This could be a plus or a minus depending upon what happens with the North American economy.

For me, the overall situation is currently too risky for an income idea.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia Has A Cash Problem
data.description:
As hyperscalers and LLM companies spend billions of dollars on new chips to place into their data centers, NVIDIA (NVDA) is cashing in big time from the AI craze.

While NVIDIA has been extremely successful in bringing its products to market just at a time when AI chatbots started to explode in popularity, NVIDIA now has to deal with another problem: it has way too much cash, and it doesn’t know what to do with it.

Cash needs to be invested in the business at high returns on equity in order to prove to the market that it still has ample internal investment opportunities. If cash just stacks on a business’s balance sheet, as it does in the case of NVIDIA, it raises questions about its capital allocation practices and whether or not the company’s high-growth period is already over.

Companies that tend to have a lot of cash on their balance sheets also risk getting pushed into pursuing share repurchases at unreasonable prices. Share buybacks are potentially accretive if they are executed below book value, which obviously is not the case for NVIDIA. Unless NVIDIA finds a way to put its cash to work at higher returns, I see headwinds for the shares in 2026.

Selling GPUs Is Profitable, But What To Do With The Cash?

The data center is where NVIDIA is making its money, and quite a bit of it. NVIDIA is selling Blackwell Graphics Processing Units to data centers, and the business has done so with quite some success. In Q326 NVIDIA generated $51.2 billion in net revenue from selling GPUs in the data center segment. NVIDIA’s GPU-selling segment increased its revenue by $10.1 billion QoQ in Q326, which is due to the successful ramp-up and new shipments of Blackwell GPUs.

According to NVIDIA, the company sees itself still very much at the beginning of the current AI revolution, with agentic AI and physical AI (robots) being the ultimate catalysts for artificial intelligence adoption across the economy. NVIDIA expects exponential growth to be driven by agentic and physical AI.

In the foreseeable future, however, NVIDIA will make the lion's share of its money from selling GPUs. In Q326, 90% of all net revenue came from selling products to data centers.

While NVIDIA still has years of growth left in the tank, the AI chip business is starting to see new challenges, such as what to do with all the cash that it is generating from selling its GPUs.

Nvidia had about $60.1 billion in cash (up $17.4 billion since the end of the 2025 year) just sitting on its balance sheet, which shows that the AI chip business may not have many attractive capital investment opportunities internally. Note, NVIDIA’s cash balance has steadily increased since 2022 as the company found major success with its AI chips for data centers.

NVIDIA uses its cash mainly to repurchase shares, but it is not doing so at great prices. NVIDIA’s NTM P/E ratio is 42.0, which means you are paying 42 times expected earnings for one dollar of NVIDIA earnings.

In the third quarter, NVIDIA repurchased $12.7 billion worth of its own shares, and the repurchase didn’t even make much of a dent in NVIDIA’s cash accounts. Since Q226 NVIDIA’s cash balance has risen $3.8 billion, despite executing almost $13 billion in buybacks. In the entire three quarters of the current fiscal year 2025, Nvidia spent $36.3 billion on share repurchases, or about $12.1 billion on average each month.

I am not convinced that paying 42 NTM earnings is a great bargain for investors, and unless NVIDIA finds higher ROE investment opportunities, the business’s stash of cash could be turning out to be a drag on its earnings growth in 2026.

NVIDIA Needs To Find New Investment Opportunities With High ROEs

I don’t believe that the biggest challenge for the chip business will be to sustain its shipment momentum for products like the Blackwell GPU or server-rack solutions such as the Grace Blackwell NVL 72. NVIDIA is shipping millions of Blackwell GPUs a year now and will do the same with the Ruben GPU that is slated for release next year.

The biggest challenge for NVIDIA will be to invest money with high returns on equity consistently, which is ultimately what matters most to the market. NVIDIA is currently seeing an uplift in returns on equity, which reflects the massive demand for its GPUs. However, if the business fails to funnel new cash into new investment opportunities, it may risk of seeing falling ROEs in the future.

NVIDIA could drive ROE upside in 2026 by:

  • Investing available cash into new AI partnerships that cement durable strategic benefits in the GPUaaS market;
  • Acquiring new AI technology and startups via its venture arm;
  • Becoming more selective when the company executes share buybacks;
  • Utilizing cash to pay a higher dividend, which is something shareholders would surely appreciate;
  • Pursuing acquisition opportunities in the realm of physical AI software and sovereign AI cloud services.

NVIDIA did make a number of high-profile investments in 2025, however, including $100 billion that was invested into OpenAI as well as $5.0 billion invested into Intel (INTC), so the AI business has the option to utilize its cash balance in strategic ways.

According to Pitchbook data, between January and mid-November, NVIDIA invested $23.7 billion in a total of 59 transactions. NVIDIA’s deal-making picked up noticeable steam in 2023 when its cash coffers started to swell from surging sales of data center chips.

While some of the investments have been criticized due to the inherent circularity of the deals (e.g. NVIDIA invests in OpenAI which uses the money to buy chips from the company), there are just not that many attractive, big deals out there, and AI companies are, generally speaking, expensive.

NVIDIA does have a big stake in CoreWeave, however, in which the chip company owns 24.2 million shares, valued at $2.0 billion. It is likely that management will continue to work closely with GPUaaS companies (and invest in them) in order to build out their AI infrastructure offers, but the question of how to deploy $60 billion effectively still lingers.

My Game Plan And Fair Value Estimate

NVIDIA has a Price-To-Sales Ratio of 13.3 vs. 5.7 for Nebius (NBIS) and 2.8 for CoreWeave. NVIDIA is also the only business here that can be valued with a traditional P/E as both Nebius and CoreWeave are not profitable. NVIDIA, however, is a preferred partner for both GPUaaS businesses, and the company gives both businesses early access to new Graphics Processing Units, which means all companies discussed here benefit from growing demand for chips, they just benefit differently.

NVIDIA sells GPUs to both companies and data centers, which in turn rent access to computing power to their own clients. NVIDIA is the ultimate beneficiary of growing demand for LLM-capable chips, while companies like Nebius and CoreWeave benefit by leasing them under their own respective GPUaaS models.

NVIDIA is obviously trading at a big premium here compared to peers, which reflects the business’s strong execution in the GPU market at a time of heightened demand. In my opinion, NVIDIA could be priced at 23-25 times FWD earnings (=Fair Value Estimate of $172-187 a share), which I believe would reflect the business’s strong GPU product portfolio as well as surging demand for Blackwell GPUs.

Since shares now already trade at 23 FWD earnings, I believe the upside is currently constrained, and I would not risk a position in NVIDIA at today’s prices. However, I would definitely consider buying NVIDIA on a pullback, say below $150 a share. This would allow for a higher margin of safety and possibly improve my risk equation quite a bit.

Suitability, Risks To Consider

Nvidia is a multi-product AI company with considerable investments in software and hardware products, which broadens the appeal of the chip company. It offers the entire software AI stack as well as critical hardware products, like its GPU suite, to serve the enterprise and consumer end markets.

However, Nvidia is not utilizing its cash effectively, in my opinion. Hoarding a lot of cash is set to be a drag on NVIDIA’s earnings growth, which may become a major challenge for the business in 2026. Capital always needs to be invested in order to earn an adequate, risk-adjusted return for investors. If cash sits idly on a business’s balance sheet and is not deployed for the benefit of shareholders, NVIDIA might not see many attractive investment opportunities itself.

My Verdict: Hold

Nvidia is growing at a dizzying pace, but while it is a good problem to have to be awash in cash, it also creates problems: NVIDIA will have to show investors that it can invest its cash at high internal rates of return to generate more shareholder value in the future or risk slowing earnings growth.

If Nvidia fails to deploy more cash into high-return opportunities, the business may continue to sit on billions of cash that are earning only a meager return. I also doubt whether share buybacks at a 42 NTM P/E ratio are really a good use of shareholder funds.

A better use of capital might be to acquire new startups with unique AI applications in order to complement NVIDIA’s existing data center business or broaden its appeal by striking major partnerships with robotics companies in order to rapidly scale physical AI products.

If NVIDIA finds higher-return investment opportunities to deploy its cash against in 2026, I can see a path for a higher growth premium. As long as NVIDIA sits on $60 billion worth of cash, and growing, I believe a buy rating at this moment in time is not deserved.

data.title: Amazon: A Correction Under $200 May Be Necessary Before Uptrend Resumes (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
I haven't mentioned Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) (AMZN:CA) in a while on Seeking Alpha. Personally, I have avoided the stock for years because of its overvaluation problem (writing mostly bearish takes, explaining the risk side of trading AMZN). To a degree, I am at it again in this article, explaining the odds of a short-term correction before a rebound can materialize later in 2026.

In terms of vindication for investing elsewhere, Amazon has severely underperformed both the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 indexes for a total return over the past 12-month and 5-year periods. Below are comparisons matching Amazon against owning the peer Big Tech Invesco QQQ (QQQ) and SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) ETFs.

However, I will say the valuation picture has improved slightly going into 2026. Today, both Walmart (WMT) here and Costco (COST) here appear to be more overvalued than Amazon in the major U.S. retailing sector. So, with better projected growth rates than peers, I am going to actually upgrade my long-term rating to Hold.

For a fundamental analysis summary of my upgrade decision, 2026's estimated P/E of 29x on an expected growth curve for EPS of 15% annually produces a forward PEG ratio under 2.0x. Not exactly a bargain basement setting, but perhaps the most acceptable valuation backstop for the stock since the end of 2022.

However, I do find serious problems in the technical trading pattern, which have been compounding since July. A rare (for Amazon) momentum downturn may be signaling a price drop below $200 into early 2026 as the next minor move.

Consequently, I would wait for a -10% to -20% correction to play out before committing new capital to AMZN.

Let me explain what I am looking at, creating the need for patience from shareholders and new buyers of the stock.

Technical Weakness Warning

Since October, a variety of unusual technical circumstances have come into view. Basically, lagging relative price action vs. the NASDAQ 100 over the trailing period of one year and a downturn in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, in conjunction with incredible weakness in the 14-day Ease of Movement indicator (reaching or close to all-time lows at the time), have only appeared twice over the past 20 years.

The two previous instances of these indicators turning lower at the same moment were in early 2014 and 2022. Both of the bearish technical trading backgrounds preceded greater levels of selling and price decline in Amazon. 2014 proved the mild selloff example, while 2022 was more of the investor wipeout case.

2025 Trading History

Below is a 12-month chart of where we stand today. You will notice relative strength vs. QQQ peaked last February, the Acc/Dist Line rolled over in July, and the EMV sell signal appeared a few weeks ago in November (circled in gold). The EMV reading essentially tied the all-time low number from March. I would note: AMZN has steadily underperformed the NASDAQ 100 since the March EMV record low.

2014 & 2022 Parallels

If you go back through the last two decades, late 2025's downward direction in the above-mentioned indicators just doesn't happen very often. Either Amazon has been outperforming the market, or the stock has been under real accumulation, or shorter-term money inflows have been evident in the EMV calculation.

Yet, in early 2014, the stock experienced a significant price dump, triggering new all-time lows in the EMV during February and April (circled in gold below). The Acc/Dist Line was generally trending higher during this period but did exhibit a downturn in March-April. From that point, the stock would zigzag lower the rest of 2014 into January 2015, with bottoms in price during May, October, and January around -15% lower.

Late 2020 into November 2021 experienced a flatlining AMZN share quote with increasingly weaker relative strength vs. peer Big Tech names, on top of rotten Acc/Dist Line action into January 2022. At that point, an imbalance of selling vs. buying volumes appeared as an all-time low EMV reading. New all-time lows in the EMV were then successively reached in March and again in May (all circled in gold below). Following the February rebound in price, Amazon would decline -50% in price into January 2023's eventual bottom.

I will say today's setup for these indicators is not as wickedly bad as early 2022 but does look materially worse than the 2014 instance. Perhaps we get a price downmove in between these two examples, in the -15% to -20% range for AMZN over the next 3-4 months, as a guesstimate.

Final Thoughts

For a review, if Amazon's price does fall under $200 in a -15% corrective move in the first quarter of 2026, a much stronger buy proposition may open for savvy investors. For a target price as technical support, $194 would fill a daily trading gap from May 2025.

Assuming operating business growth forecasts remain unchanged long term (for example, we avoid a recession in consumer/business spending next year), a sub-$200 quote could bring a forward PEG ratio closer to 1.6x to 1.8x. PEG scores closer to 1.0x are historically considered Strong Buy territory by Wall Street in any equity. In addition, if the "risk-free" Treasury yield curve drops next year to rates well under 4%, Amazon's earnings yield potentially above 4% by 2027 (P/E under 25x) may offer better support for shareholder worth.

The technical trading evidence is suggesting a weaker underpinning for the stock now exists vs. what Amazon has typically witnessed in the past. Plus, the U.S. equity market in general continues to trade at an unsustainable valuation level (in my view, trading the markets for almost 40 years).

Heavy insider selling at Amazon is continuing in late 2025, highlighting that management is anxious to liquidate at least a portion of their holdings to lock in gains. If there's any silver lining, it's the fact that sell blocks over the past three months have primarily been in the millions of dollars from managers and hundreds of millions from founder Jeff Bezos (not billions of dollars).

One bullish scenario I am running through my research is that Amazon may be able to "outperform" the NASDAQ 100 and S&P 500 indexes for a number of years after a minor -10% to -20% correction phase appears soon. I might even put a Buy rating on the stock if a serious drawdown becomes reality in 2026.

For aggressive traders, a short-term sell or short position is one option. However, I do feel the Amazon valuation could support a move above $300 in coming years. Another possibility: AMZN's price may flatline in the low-$200s for some time, as the overall Wall Street health condition becomes more precarious with the vast majority of equities falling in price.

When I bring all of the pros (improving fundamental valuation) and cons (bearish momentum) together, any outlook or forecast rating beyond six months may be best put as "Neutral" or "Hold." I would wait for price weakness before buying a new position or adding to your Amazon stake. However, rushing to sell may not prove the wisest course of action, especially if you are holding large taxable gains. A prudent middle-ground strategy may include selling covered calls as a price hedge and income generator.

At least, that's the way I see Amazon's position for investors currently.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: ISCF: Japan-Heavy ETF With Banner 2025, Yet Skeptical Stance Is Warranted For 2026
data.description:
Today, I would like to initiate coverage of the iShares International Small-Cap Equity Factor ETF (ISCF), a passively managed exchange-traded fund offering exposure to developed-world small-cap equities selected using a sophisticated factor-centered process, with a Hold rating.

The fact is, the Japan- and UK-heavy ISCF is wrapping up 2025 on an extremely optimistic note, beating the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) by about 14.9%, in part supported by the impressive performance of the euro, the pound sterling, the Australian dollar, etc.

This is a beautiful example of how being a contrarian (and venturing into the high-risk universe of developed-world small caps amid the U.S. market rally is a perfect example of non-mainstream thinking) may actually reward investors.

However, my goal today is not to talk about how generous ISCF's returns have been in 2025. The question is what the year 2026 will bring for international small caps, and I would dare to assume that the case for ISCF (and for its peers as well) beating the S&P 500 ETF for the second year in a row is implausible, as the data to construct an overly optimistic hypothesis like that is lacking, plus I do not anticipate the forces that contributed to its return in 2025 to continue pushing it higher with a similar energy in 2026.

So below, I would like to give due attention to ISCF's strategy, performance, and portfolio composition, as well as offer some context on its peers, to explain why I am modestly skeptical about this vehicle.

ISCF Key Facts

  • Inception: April 28, 2015
  • Investment Adviser: BlackRock Fund Advisors
  • AUM: $539.87 million, as of December 24
  • Turnover: 30%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.24%

ISCF Strategy Review

As we know from the prospectus available on the iShares website, the STOXX International Small-Cap Equity Factor Index is the core of its strategy. However, an important nuance can be found in the fact sheet. The gist is that the ETF tracked a different index before March 1, 2023, namely the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Diversified Multiple-Factor Index (Net). Even though some methodological overlap can be identified between the indices (i.e., their focus on developed-world small caps and the inclusion of factor considerations related to value, momentum, and quality), they are not exactly the same, and I believe for the sake of accuracy, we need to focus on the ETF's returns since the strategy change (a quick look at the returns prior to that makes sense nonetheless).

As described in the prospectus, ISCF's underlying index comprises small-cap names from the STOXX International Developed Markets Small Cap Index that are identified and assigned weights "using an optimization process designed to maximize exposure to four target factors: momentum, quality, value, and low volatility." As we know from the STOXX website,

The STOXX International Developed Markets Small Cap Index is a broad market cap weighted index designed to represent the performance of the Small Cap companies from Developed Markets excluding US covering approximately 15% of investable market capitalization.

The U.S. equities are ignored, while the Canadian ones are welcome. An important detail I find necessary to highlight is that, as per the STOXX Country Classification, South Korea is considered an emerging market. The same is fair for the MSCI (MSCI) Market Classification. So the ETF should have had no exposure to the Korean equities when it tracked the MSCI index. However, the S&P Developed ex US Broad Market Index (Net), with which the ETF compares its returns as per page S-30 of the prospectus, does include Korean companies, and in the SPDR® Portfolio Developed World ex-US ETF (SPDW), they had a 5.24% weight as of December 22.

As to the frequency of adjustments, the STOXX International Small-Cap Equity Factor Index has a quarterly review and rebalancing schedule. The weighting process is guided by a few constraints. More specifically:

The maximum weight of a single security is 10%, and the sum of security weights that are individually greater than 4.5% must be less than 22.5% of the Underlying Index. The Index Provider also applies other constraints, such as country and sector exposures relative to the Parent Index, among others.

ISCF Portfolio Analysis

As of December 22, there were 1,097 equities in the ISCF portfolio, with the main ten accounting for 5.67% of the net assets. As we can see in the holdings dataset that is available on the iShares website, other assets in the portfolio were cash, cash collateral and margins, futures, FX, and a money market fund.

Ticker (as per the ISCF dataset) U.S. Ticker Company Sector Weight, %, as of December 22 Location
ABN (AAVMY) ABN AMRO Bank N.V. Financials 0.94 The Netherlands
SAB (BNDSY) Banco de Sabadell, S.A. Financials 0.9 Spain
BMPS (BMDPF) Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena S.p.A. Financials 0.71 Italy
LOGN (LOGI) Logitech International S.A. Information Technology 0.52 Switzerland
FTT (FINGF) Finning International Inc. Industrials 0.46 Canada
SQN (SWQGF) Swissquote Group Holding SA Financials 0.46 Switzerland
CG (CGAU) Centerra Gold Inc. Materials 0.42 Canada
DBI None Dalrymple Bay Infrastructure Limited Industrials 0.42 Australia
HBAN (HLVTY) Helvetia Baloise Holding AG Financials 0.42 Switzerland
ACLN (ACLLY) Accelleron Industries AG Industrials 0.42 Switzerland

Created by the author using data from the ETF and Seeking Alpha

To offer a glimpse into how ISCF's top holdings have been doing in 2025, I should mention that ABN AMRO Bank N.V., an Amsterdam-based bank with a market cap of about €18.1 billion, had a blockbuster year, delivering a close to 96.6% price return in Amsterdam as of writing this article. Banco de Sabadell, S.A., with a market cap of about €16.8 billion, was similarly successful, delivering an approximately 80% return in Madrid.

Regarding sectors, ISCF does have a few similarities to its peers, including the Schwab International Small-Cap Equity ETF (SCHC), the iShares MSCI EAFE Small-Cap ETF (SCZ), the SPDR® S&P® International Small Cap ETF (GWX), and the Schwab Fundamental International Small Equity ETF (FNDC), as well as SPDW, but their preferences are not entirely identical.

For example, compared to SPDW, it is significantly overweight in real estate and materials, among others, while underweight in financials and health care, to name a few. Compared to SCHC, it has more confidence in consumer discretionary and financials while underweighting a few sectors, including materials and health care.

Speaking of countries, most of its net assets are allocated to Japanese, UK, Canadian, and Australian stocks that combined account for 54.6% of the net assets. My calculations show that the top 20 countries out of 27 it has exposure to (as per the dataset) account for 98.67% of the portfolio.

The main difference from SPDW's country allocation is that ISCF ignores South Korea. Other notable differences are that ISCF is overweight in Japanese, UK, and Australian equities, while underweight in Canadian, Swiss, German, and French, among others.

ISCF Performance

As I said above, in 2025, ISCF has clearly had a moment.

It is still of note, though, that it has underperformed such peers as FNDC, GWX, and SCHC, as well as SPDW, beating only SCZ. Anyway, an over 34% total return is still impressive.

What factors helped it to beat the U.S. market I proxied with IVV? I believe the primary one was the tariff drama-related volatility that reverberated through currencies and equities, and especially the depreciation of the U.S. dollar vs. the euro, the Swiss franc, and the pound sterling. Also, propelled by the hawkishness of the Bank of Japan, the yen was a massive factor for ISCF and its Japan-heavy peers earlier this year, but its impact dropped to near zero in the months that followed.

The fact is, about 84.9% of ISCF's net assets are allocated to stocks priced in six currencies, namely the EUR, JPY, GBP, CAD, AUD, and CHF. As we can see from the performance of the Invesco CurrencyShares® Euro Currency Trust ETF (FXE), the Invesco CurrencyShares® British Pound Sterling Trust ETF (FXB), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Japanese Yen Trust ETF (FXY), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Australian Dollar Trust ETF (FXA), the Invesco CurrencyShares® Swiss Franc Trust ETF (FXF), and the Invesco CurrencyShares® Canadian Dollar Trust ETF (FXC), all these currencies have gained vs. the USD. However, I do not expect these factors to remain that supportive in 2026.

Next, zooming in on the monthly returns, we see that most of ISCF's outperformance happened in the first half of the year (February–May).

Month ISCF Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
January -0.7%
February 2.2%
March 7.0%
April 6.1%
May 0.2%
June -0.3%
July -2.6%
August 2.3%
September -1.5%
October -3.5%
November 1.2%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

And while it is ahead of IVV YTD, it has trailed it by about 2.5% in the last six months.

Second, the data for March 2023–November 2025 does not look convincing at all. On a side note, I did not add FNDC to the comparison, as it changed its index in June 2024.

We see a few issues here.

  • ISCF underperformed not only the U.S. market proxied with IVV but also SPDW and SCHC.
  • It captured about 101.65% of SPDW's downside but only 96.69% of its upside.
  • Its risk-adjusted returns measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios were softer than IVV's and SPDW's, though it did outmaneuver international small-cap peers.

Besides, while we have just 33 full calendar months of ISCF's performance with the new index, we can take a look at its performance prior to the change, since, as I said above, there is some methodological overlap between the indices.

So my calculations show that over May 2015–February 2023, ISCF delivered a 5.37% annualized total return, close to 2x lower than IVV. Still, it managed to outmaneuver SPDW, which delivered just 3.74% CAGR.

Moreover, nothing can hinder us from taking a look at other international small-cap plays like SCHC, which has a much longer history going back to the post-GFC global markets recovery. The fact is its returns before 2025 do not look convincing at all. For instance, it had seven years of underperformance in a row (2018–2024), and since its inception in 2010, it has never had two consecutive years of outperformance. It beat IVV only in February–December 2010, 2012, 2015, 2017, and January–November 2025.

Year SCHC Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
February–December 2010 8.80%
2011 -18.73%
2012 3.13%
2013 -10.90%
2014 -20.83%
2015 1.00%
2016 -9.17%
2017 7.63%
2018 -14.16%
2019 -8.17%
2020 -7.93%
2021 -16.73%
2022 -3.58%
2023 -11.97%
2024 -22.95%
January–November 2025 17.12%

Prepared by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

As of writing this article on December 24, I see that SCHC is beating IVV by close to 19% in 2025. In fact, this is its best year since its inception.

So my point here is that considering no new phase of the USD depreciation (and I do not expect one), there is little to no possibility of ISCF delivering a higher total return than IVV's in 2026.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, with its multifactor take on developed-world small caps, ISCF has been on a tear in 2025, beating not only SCZ but also the S&P 500 ETF. I admit that this is a decent momentum story, but thinking that 2026 should be another alpha year is counterintuitive, and there is no historical data to back up this assumption. I also do not expect the FX factor to be as supportive as in 2025. So more likely its performance will revert back to what we saw in 2023 and 2024. What should not change, though, is ISCF's high downside capture ratio.

data.title: TJX Companies: Rating Downgrade On Expensive Valuation
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating for The TJX Companies (TJX) two years ago in April '24, as I saw potential for TJX to beat its FY25 guidance and that it would drive multiples higher. Fast forward to today, the fundamental story has gotten better. HomeGoods has pivoted toward frequency, the customer base is skewing younger, and international expansion is going well. Fundamentally, the business is stronger than it was two years ago. The question is whether the current valuation already reflects most of that, and I think it already has. I downgrade to hold.

The Pivot to Frequency is Working (HomeGoods)

One very interesting point I observed with TJX since then was that the HomeGoods banner had shifted away from a discretionary and cyclical shopping destination toward something that will capture consumer traffic all year round. For perspective, in the latest Q3 2026 quarter, TJX managed to deliver 5% CSG. And I find this very bullish because this is a quarter that is typically quiet for the home category. Remember that in Q3, there is no spring renovation benefit and also no holiday gifting tailwind in Q3, so when HomeGoods posts that level of CSG, we know for a fact that this banner is seeing demand from elsewhere.

My belief is that the mix shift toward consumables, snacks, and giftable items is delivering as expected. I really find this to be a smart strategy because these categories pull customers in more frequently and for smaller, repeat purchases. Instead of waiting for the occasion where a consumer needs to buy a new couch or renovate their home, which are very infrequent occasions, having these new, smaller consumable items gives shoppers a reason to stop by more frequently (even daily). The general idea here is that, over time, customer lifetime value for this HomeGoods banner improves because more visits mean a higher likelihood of a purchase. It also helps with stabilizing the revenue profile since customers are coming in more frequently.

The knock-on impact from these is also that valuation should get better. My view is that home retailers have typically traded at a discount because revenue tends to be lumpy and tied to housing cycles. That was the old HomeGoods banner. But with the shift now, HomeGoods has a steadier demand pattern profile, which allows for better inventory planning and labor utilization (it's easier to plan how many labors needed for each store). All of these should lead to the market rewarding the stock with a higher multiple.

Capturing the Youth

In my view, the long-term growth for TJX is really dependent on whether it can successfully pivot the brand to capture the younger crowd. This is especially true for TJX because this is a brand with a long operating history, which means the bulk of its customers have aged together with the brand. If the customer base eventually ages out, the “terminal value” for TJX gets impaired since the brand could become irrelevant.

The good news is that TJX has managed to pivot. Management has been explicit that customer acquisition continues to skew toward Gen Z and Millennials. This is the most important data point that we need to prove that TJX is lowering the “age” of its customer base, and that itself materially lowers the long-term risk profile. The “how” matters here because it shows why this pivot is structural. What management did was to adjust its marketing strategy. Instead of recycling marketing at the same seasonal spots, they have reinvested in channels that over-index with younger consumers. A few good examples are sponsorships tied to women's sports and targeted podcast advertising. And I think the logic makes sense here because, as TJX, you want to introduce the off-price value proposition early (when consumers are young) so that their spending habits will include purchasing off-price items. Once TJX can help form that habit early, it effectively captures a customer for the many years to come.

Global Growth, the New Growth Lever

TJX's international expansion is a growth driver that one shouldn't ignore. In the latest call, management confirmed plans to enter Spain with the TK Maxx banner in early 2026. We've already seen the concept work in the UK, Ireland, Germany, and Poland. But Spain's outlet-centre footprint per capita is below more developed European off-price markets like the UK and Italy, and that gives TJX a longer runway to capture “new demand” without competing with incumbents. Also, Spain would be the first Western European country for TJX. If this concept works, it makes the case for broader Western European expansion more plausible.

There is also a less obvious benefit that matters, and that is vendor relationships. With a wider international footprint, it allows TJX to clear inventory on a global scale. This is a huge advantage against local sub-scale players because it would be a position of strength when it comes to acquiring excess merchandise, particularly from international brands looking to clear high-volume inventories. Over time, with more countries, it means deeper vendor ties, which gives TJX better access to product. This is the same flywheel effect we see in the US, where more stores create more buying power. More buying power improves inventory availability. Better inventory drives traffic and transaction growth. That loop becomes harder to replicate as TJX scales.

Valuation

All is good from a fundamental basis, but the valuation scares me. TJX is trading at 30x forward PE, which is close to the largest premium vs. peers. And this is despite TJX's revenue growth outlook being similar to Ross Stores (ROST), which is at a high-single-digit percentage. But ROSS trades at 25x forward PE. I get the attractiveness of TJX penetrating the younger generation consumer cohort and Spain expansion, but at 30x, the upside doesn't seem attractive to me. Let's just take consensus FY28 adj. EPS estimates to anchor expectations. They estimate an adj. EPS of $5.73. At 30x, that equates to a share price of $171, a 10% upside over 2 years (~5% a year).

Conclusion

My view for TJX is now a hold. It is executing well by addressing HomeGoods issue, capturing younger age demographics, and penetrating global markets. However, at ~30x forward earnings, much of that upside looks priced in. Using consensus FY28 adjusted EPS of $5.73, the implied return is not attractive at all.

data.title: AAR: Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
In August, I concluded that shares of AAR (AIR) were cruising to fresh highs. The company was delivering strong revenue and earnings growth following the integration of the acquisition of Triumph.

With elevated leverage rapidly coming down, there are some drivers for continued earnings growth in 2026, and even as shares were trading near their all-time highs, more momentum was seen.

Momentum and news flow quickly arrived in the fall, after AAR announced no less than 3 deals while offering some equity to keep leverage in check as well. Such moves and continued operating momentum make me happy to maintain a position, even at current higher levels.

Aviation Aftermarket Services

AAR provides aviation aftermarket services, with the business founded soon after World War II. Such products and services include parts, integrated solutions, expeditionary services, and repair & engineering services.

Positioned as an independent provider of such services, the company employs over 6,000 workers who provide such services and products across the globe. AAR serves commercial carriers as well as defense and governmental agencies, although the commercial market makes up about two-thirds of sales.

By now the company generates about $2.7 billion in sales, with parts supply being the largest segment, making up about 40% of sales. This is largely complemented by repair & engineering and integrated solutions, with expeditionary services having become a minimal unit with the passage of time. The repair & engineering units have seen a considerable bump following a $725 million deal to acquire Triumph, a transaction announced late in 2023.

The company is operating in a massive, nearly $140 billion global MRO market, which provides continued growth and consolidation opportunities, coming on top of solid growth achievements already delivered. Over the past decade, the company has nearly doubled sales, with these sales obviously taking a dip during the pandemic. Operating margins have risen from mid-single digits to high single digits, all amidst a pretty stable share count.

About The Valuation

In July of this year, AAR reported a 20% increase in fiscal 2025 sales to $2.78 billion, as EBITDA was up 34% to $324 million. Net debt was reported at $880 million, for a leverage ratio reported at 2.7 times. All this allowed for adjusted earnings of $3.91 per share.

While no formal 2026 guidance was issued, the company struck an upbeat tone, as the company announced a $15 million deal to acquire Aerostrat in August, adding long-range maintenance planning software, as this was truly a bolt-on transaction.

Trading at $77 in August, shares traded at 19 times trailing earnings, which were seen advancing further in 2026, certainly as the company has been incurring substantial interest expenses on its debt load. This could allow for real deleveraging amidst debt refinancing and debt reduction. I was working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share earnings number in the fiscal year 2026. With leverage seen in the low 2s, and earnings multiples seen at 17-18 times, shares looked fairly valued, with potential for shares to continue to fly higher going forward.

Eventful Times

Since August, it has been busy on the corporate front for AAR. In September, the company reported a solid 12% increase in first-quarter sales to $740 million. Margins kept improving, with EBITDA up 18% to $87 million, as adjusted earnings rose some 27% to $1.08 per share. Net debt actually ticked up to $942 million, yet with EBITDA trending around $337 million, that was not a concern, as the strong start made me feel really comfortable with the earnings numbers mentioned above.

Days later, AAR announced the acquisition of American Distributors Holding, a distributor of components and assemblies, in a $146 million cash deal. Founded in 1983, ADI employs about 400 workers, who generate $149 million in sales and $15 million in EBITDA, revealing that a 1 times sales multiple and nearly 10 times EBITDA multiple have been paid.

With pro forma net debt seen up to $1.09 billion, the company sold 3 million shares towards the end of the month. At an offer price of $83 per share, this yields a quarter of a billion in gross proceeds, for pro forma net debt around $850 million. Note that some dilution will be incurred, with the transaction seen increasing the count from about 36 million shares to 39 million shares, yet it allows for the purchase of ADI and net deleveraging at the same time.

Aided by this influx, AAR announced the purchase of HAECO Americas in a $78 million cash deal, adding to the repair & engineering segment, although no further financial details have been announced. Just before the year end, AAR announced a $35 million cash deal to acquire Aircraft Reconfig Technologies to expand engineering and certification capabilities, with again no financial details being announced.

In between the three deals and the share issuance, I peg the net debt load at just over $950 million, as I see leverage ratios in the mid-2s. The company has incurred about 8% dilution here, but the ADI deal alone will boost sales by about 5%, with an unknown revenue contribution from the most recent two deals.

And Now?

Following an acquisitive period, which seems to have been paid for with higher-valued shares, I think that AAR is doing just fine here. Shares have seen further gains, now trading at $85 per share, trading within imminent sight of the highs around $90 per share.

Following the strong start to the year and the M&A moves over the fall, I feel very comfortable working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share estimate for the year, potentially seeing upside to that. This now pushes up forward earnings multiples to 19-20 times earnings, with upside to positive delivery. For all that, I anxiously look forward to learning more about the second quarter earnings report.

Hence, I continue to see AAR operating from a position of strength and applaud this. Having held a small position, I am backing the recent moves made, while the business itself does just fine, making me a patient holder of the stock.

data.title: Immatics: PRAME Leader's Transition To Commercial Reality
data.description:
My optimistic view on Immatics (IMTX) is driven by the progress of the company's three-wave strategy. What we are seeing is the execution of a systematic approach to commercializing the company's products: Wave one establishes a foothold in the treatment of melanoma with anzu-cel; Wave two expands the company's presence into other types of cancers that are difficult to treat, such as ovarian cancer, using the next generation of cell therapy products called IMA203CD8; and Wave three makes TCR therapeutics available to all patients by introducing "off-the-shelf" bispecific products such as IMA402. I believe that the fact that Immatics is currently enrolling patients in a Phase 3 trial and has sufficient cash reserves to last until the company launches commercially provides significant risk reduction relative to its peer group and offers a compelling investment opportunity for investors who are willing to look beyond the short-term volatility associated with investing in biotech companies.

Current Financial Health

As of September 30, 2025, Immatics has a healthy amount of cash on hand; a sum of $505.8 million of cash, cash equivalents, and other financial instruments. Based upon the company’s guidance, this cash will allow Immatics to continue operating into the second half of 2027. This timeframe is important because it encompasses both the Biologics License Application submission scheduled for the first half of 2027 and the subsequent commercial launch later in the same year. Therefore, the company will have sufficient funds available throughout its initial commercial milestone.

An analysis of the income statement demonstrates that total revenue for the quarter was $6.1 million. At first glance, this appears to be a dramatic decline in comparison to the $59.4 million of revenue that Immatics recorded in the same quarter of 2024. Context, however, is essential to understanding the data presented above. The previous year’s revenue included a non-recurring revenue recognition event resulting from the termination of the IMA401 collaboration between Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) and Immatics. The revenue generated in Q3 2025 reflects a normalized rate of growth from the ongoing but much smaller collaborations with which Immatics is currently involved. While gaining back full rights to IMA401 could potentially add value to the company’s business model, as it would give Immatics the opportunity to make decisions regarding the combinations of their bispecific products, the decline in revenue is clearly not indicative of a weakness in the business model.

On the expense side of the balance sheet, R&D spending increased to $55.4 million as compared to $45.7 million in the same quarter of the prior year. The company is using the large amounts of capital it has raised to advance its clinical trials, including the SUPRAME Phase 3 trial and the expansion of the bispecific cohorts. G&A expenses were modestly higher at $14.9 million due to the natural increase in pre-commercial spending that occurs as companies begin to prepare for the launch of new products. The reported net loss of $59.3 million for the quarter is consistent with the level of spending that the company has incurred in terms of its operational tempo. With the existing burn rate and the sizeable amount of cash available to fund operations, Immatics is able to create a financial safety net that enables the company to engage in negotiations with potential partners or financiers without appearing to be desperate.

PRAME and Clinical Pipeline

The core of the Immatics investment thesis is focused on its PRAME franchise and is unfolding in three stages. The first stage will be driven by the launch of anzutresgene autoleucel, or anzu-cel (formerly IMA203), an autologous TCR-T cell therapy targeted at patients suffering from metastatic melanoma. The Phase 3 SUPRAME trial is now underway where anzu-cel will be compared to investigator’s choice in patients with unresectable or metastatic cutaneous melanoma who have previously been treated with checkpoint inhibitors. This trial will provide the required data to achieve the company’s first regulatory approval.

The second wave of products in the pipeline includes IMA203CD8, a second generation of cell therapy. In contrast to anzu-cel, IMA203CD8 involves the co-transduction of CD8 along with the PRAME TCR. As a result, CD4+ T cells can assist in the attack on the tumor by recruiting them to the site. By using two different types of T cells instead of one, the expectation is that the efficacy of the product will be increased as well as the duration of the response. Historically, solid tumors have proven to be less responsive to immunotherapies than melanoma, and therefore this is expected to enable Immatics to expand its target population beyond melanoma to include a broad spectrum of tumor types, including gynecological cancers such as ovarian and uterine carcinoma.

The third wave of products includes the TCR Bispecifics, specifically IMA402 and IMA401. Similar to monoclonal antibodies, these are off-the-shelf biologics that function to bind to both a patient’s T cells and the tumor cells, thereby acting as a “glue.” Compared to autologous cell therapies, there is a significant advantage to this approach. In order to produce an autologous cell therapy, each patient requires the production of a separate product. In contrast, a biologic can be produced in large quantities and stored in freezers. Once thawed, the biologic is ready for administration. Recent clinical results have demonstrated that Immatics is capable of translating their TCR experience to a bi-specific format, while maintaining the affinity and specificity of the TCR and minimizing the toxicity issues that commonly occur when using bi-specific agents.

Recent Clinical Updates

Anzu-cel has a validated Phase 1b treatment for metastatic uveal melanoma based upon the updated data that were presented at ESMO 2025. Of particular note is the fact that anzu-cel had a confirmed objective response rate of 67% (of 16 patients), which is a notable achievement since it has been proven to be very difficult to treat metastatic uveal melanoma; additionally, the response rate was generated in a population that was heavily pretreated. The median duration of response was 11.0 months, and although the median overall survival had not been reached at a median follow-up of 14.3 months, it suggests that anzu-cel provides durable benefits as opposed to merely transient tumor shrinkage. Therefore, the data supporting the potential of anzu-cel led to the start of a dedicated Phase 2 cohort for uveal melanoma; the data also demonstrates management's commitment to this specific indication.

Although the data supporting the potential of anzu-cel are clearly important for the long-term growth story of the company, perhaps even more important for the long-term growth story is the update announced by the company on December 11, 2025, regarding IMA203CD8. One of the primary concerns with TCR-T therapies is that they may not function well in cancers outside of those that are highly immunogenic, such as melanoma. However, the data presented at the ESMO Immuno-Oncology Congress 2025 clearly address this concern. The data support the idea that IMA203CD8 is functioning in ovarian carcinoma, a tumor type that historically has been difficult to treat with immunotherapy. At doses of 2.3 billion cells or higher, the company reported 2 confirmed partial responses and 1 ongoing metabolic complete response among 5 patients.

The safety profile of IMA203CD8 remains manageable; specifically, there were no grade 5 adverse events associated with the therapy; the company is currently investigating protocols that would eliminate the need for post-infusion low-dose IL-2. If the need for post-infusion low-dose IL-2 could be eliminated, it would likely improve the patient experience and safety profile of the therapy, thus improving the attractiveness of the therapy to practicing oncologists in the community setting.

Counterarguments and Risks

For Immatics, the major challenge to its ability to generate revenue from the sale of an autologous cell therapy (anzu-cel) will be the logistics involved in making it commercially viable. In contrast to selling a pill or a vial of drug product, anzu-cel involves collecting the patient’s cells; transporting the collected cells to a manufacturing facility where they are genetically modified; and then returning the genetically modified cells to the patient. Although Immatics has built a dedicated 100,000 square foot manufacturing facility in Houston to facilitate this process, there is still a significant operational execution risk associated with manufacturing and vein-to-vein delivery times. Therefore, if either manufacturing or vein-to-vein delivery times are delayed, then the ability to successfully launch anzu-cel in the marketplace may be limited by those delays regardless of the efficacy of the drug.

SUPRAME Phase 3 is an event-driven study; therefore, the timeline for completion of the data analysis of the trial results will depend upon how quickly patients experience disease progression or die. Therefore, while Immatics anticipates completing the first interim analysis and final analysis in 2026, biological variability makes it difficult to predict when the actual date of the first interim analysis and/or final analysis will occur. If the actual date of the first interim analysis and/or final analysis is later than expected, then the timing of the BLA filing and cash flow generation will be later than anticipated, which may require a capital raise prior to the end of 2027.

Addressable Market and Competitive Landscape

In the U.S. and the EU5 countries (France, Germany, Italy, Spain, United Kingdom), there are approximately 9,000 patients per year that suffer from PRAME-positive, HLA-A*02:01 positive cutaneous and uveal melanoma. Although this is a niche market, it is a high value niche and sets up the commercial infrastructure.

As we grow beyond this first market, the next step is with IMA203CD8, targeting gynecological cancers and other solid tumors; the number of patients addressed grows dramatically. The company believes that when all indications (such as ovarian, uterine, and non-small cell lung cancer) are included in the cell therapy franchise, the number of patients addressed in the U.S. and EU5 would be in excess of 75,000 per year. At this point, the revenue potential moves from hundreds of millions to billions.

The bispecifics provide the greatest breadth of application. Because they can be used in the earlier lines of therapy and potentially in conjunction with checkpoint inhibitors, IMA402 provides access to a patient population of over 145,000 patients per year in the same geographies. Additionally, the combination of IMA401 (MAGEA4/8) and IMA402 (PRAME) in squamous non-small cell lung cancer offers a significant opportunity. Data indicates that over 90% of sqNSCLC patients are positive for either PRAME or MAGEA4/8, and approximately 60% of patients are positive for both of these antigens. This dual-targeting approach may provide access to a patient population of approximately 40,000 patients per year, and represents a comprehensive solution for this difficult-to-treat indication.

Competitively, while companies such as Immunocore (IMCR) have a commercial product (tebentafusp) for uveal melanoma, Immatics' dominance in the PRAME space is unique. Tebentafusp is limited to gp100-positive patients, and has a different mechanism than anzu-cel. Immatics' anzu-cel has demonstrated efficacy in patients who have progressed after treatment with tebentafusp, indicating non-overlapping utility. Immatics is the most advanced in the PRAME space with the broadest portfolio, establishing a defensive moat around this high-value target.

Future Outlook and Conclusion

While there are risks associated with manufacturing logistics and competition, Immatics has taken steps to address these issues through its investment in its Houston facility, and the unique characteristics of its T-cell receptor bispecific drugs, which provide a differentiator from competing treatments. Therefore, for investors who wish to participate in the next generation of breakthroughs in immuno-oncology, and who require both near-term commercialization visibility and long-term optionality in their portfolio, Immatics provides a viable alternative. As such, the data indicates that Immatics' vision to have a positive, life-changing impact on cancer patients is rapidly being realized, and it is reasonable to believe that Immatics' valuation will reflect this maturation as the company moves toward its 2027 product launch.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 12/25/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending: Trades At A Deep Discount For A Reason
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies were one of the best sectors to collect a high dividend yield from only a few years ago. As the interest rate environment remains elevated, BDCs have struggled to grow their portfolios and earnings as portfolio companies feel the burden of a higher cost of debt. Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. (NCDL) is a BDC I haven't covered before, so I wanted to take some time to assess its value proposition as we approach the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NCDL's share price has declined by about 18.15%. So as the debt market continues to face headwinds, NCDL has struggled to regain positive growth momentum. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 7.92% over the same time frame. Following the declines, NCDL now offers a very generous dividend yield of 13%. However, I remain very cautious about the sustainability of the distributions based on the latest earnings results. It appears that the portfolio is struggling to generate earnings that exceed the distribution amounts.

NCDL now trades at one of its deepest discounts to NAV valuations ever, but I believe this is a result of the underlying challenges that it faces. The portfolio has failed to grow its earnings, and management struggles to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. As a result, the underlying net asset value continues to erode quarter after quarter. Therefore, I remain cautious about entry here and believe that investors should remain on the sidelines until momentum starts to shift. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NCDL implements within its portfolio.

Q3 Earnings

Unlike traditional equities, NCDL generates its earnings by investing in a diverse pool of debt investments. NCDL essentially lends capital to borrowers and collects interest or equity in exchange. So to succeed, NCDL is relying on a borrower's ability to keep up with its required debt maintenance. According to the latest portfolio breakdown, NCDL has investments at a total fair value of $1.96B that are spread across 213 different portfolio companies. In order to reduce risks, NCDL structures the majority of its investments as first lien debt, which sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. The portfolio's structure by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 89.8%
  • Subordinated Debt: 8.1%
  • Equity: 2.1%

Expanding on this diversity, we can see that NCDL's portfolio also spans across a diverse range of industries to mitigate any sort of concentration risks. According to the latest breakdown, NCDL is most heavily allocated to Healthcare & Pharmaceutical investments at 17.6% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Services: Business and Beverage, Food, & Tobacco investments, which account for 16.5% and 7.6% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries makes up more than 41% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these areas will have the greatest impact on NCDL's total return potential.

NCDL reported its Q3 earnings in early November, and the results were mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share, which is equivalent to $21.3M during the quarter. This was a sizeable decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.58 per share. Additionally, NCDL experienced a net realized loss of $0.05 per share for the quarter, which resulted in a total net increase in net operations of $0.38 per share.

It appears that the main contributor to the lower earnings is the decline in interest income that NCDL has experienced. For instance, the amount of interest income received from portfolio companies amounted to $48.2M, which was a large decline from the prior year's interest income of $57.3M. Total investment income, including dividend, PIK interest, and other income, amounted to $51M, which was also a large decline from the prior year's total investment income of $60.2M.

As earnings decline, this is one of the clearest indications that the portfolio companies within the portfolio may be struggling to keep up with their debt payments. Additionally, it could be a sign that the selected portfolio companies are struggling to thrive under the higher interest rate environment, since 94.2% of the borrowers are structured on a floating rate basis. A higher interest rate means that portfolio companies will need to pay out a larger portion of their earnings towards servicing debt payments.

In more positive news, management has been able to reduce the internal operating expenses over the last quarter to help slow the negative impact of earnings. For instance, total expenses amounted to $29.7M during the quarter, which was a decline from the prior year's total operating expenses of $34.2M. The lower expenses can be attributed to the reduction in interest expenses and professional fees. However, management fees did considerably increase year-over-year, now totaling $5.1M.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since NCDL is structured as a BDC, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, NCDL now trades at a large discount to NAV of 22.46%. For reference, NCDL has traded at an average discount to NAV of 11.83% over the last twelve months. While this larger discount may seem like an attractive opportunity to add, I'd argue that this is a direct reflection of the challenges that NCDL is currently facing. Therefore, I do not think this is a great time to accumulate.

A big source of the troubles comes from NCDL's failure to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. For instance, NCDL experienced new investment funding of $29.2M during the latest quarter. However, this was offset by sales and repayments of $61.3M, which ultimately resulted in a net decrease in investment activity of $25.04M during the quarter. When you combine the lack of growth with the limited earnings growth, we can see that NCDL's NAV continues to decline. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $18.15 per share
  • Q4 2024: $18.18 per share
  • Q1 2025: $17.96 per share
  • Q2 2025: $17.92 per share
  • Q3 2025: $17.85 per share

As long as interest rates remain elevated, I believe that management will struggle to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. The higher cost of debt was also a reason why NCDL experienced two new non-accruals during the quarter. Non-accruals represent the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and struggling to keep up with their debt payments. According to the latest earnings call, non-accruals still remain relatively low compared to many other BDC peers.

As of September 30, nonaccruals represent just 0.4% of our total investment portfolio on a fair value basis and 0.9% on a cost basis. We believe the strength of our platform, including experienced workout and portfolio management teams will continue to drive favorable results. Portfolio diversification remains a key focus of ours within our overall investment portfolio. - Kenneth Kencel, CEO

As non-accruals increase, the overall risk profile of a BDC increases because this indicates that the underlying investments are vulnerable to current market conditions. A higher non-accrual rate means that net investment income is negatively impacted. To be fair though, many other BDCs are facing similar challenges as they see their non-accrual rate rise. When compared to peers, NCDL's non-accruals aren't that bad. For reference, here are some non-accrual rates for peer BDCs:

  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 1.3% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN): 1.2% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.45 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13%. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $0.43 per share, which is a clear indication that earnings no longer support the distributions being paid. As long as NCDL pays out more than it actually earns in income, the underlying NAV will decline, growth will remain limited, and the dividend will remain at the risk of a cut in the near-term. However, management reassured investors by stating that they feel confident in their ability to continue supporting the current dividend level.

We felt good about our ability to continue to earn, again, within $0.01 or $0.02. Obviously, we under-earned by $0.02 this quarter our dividend of $0.45. But again, within a range, and there were some reasons for that, including the two nonaccrual names that I alluded to in terms of reducing our earnings for this particular period. - Kenneth Kencel, CEO

Despite management's reassurance, I believe that keeping the dividend unchanged may be a mistake. By continuously paying out more than it generates in income, the NAV will continue to deteriorate if management isn't able to offset this with new investment activity. I fear that NCDL may be kicking the can down the road and may end up cutting the dividend in the future when the damage is already done. Management did confirm that there is spillover income available, but didn't specifically cover how large of a bucket this remains.

Additionally, investors should consider the fact that the distributions received from NCDL do not offer any tax efficiency. Since payouts are supported by interest income collected from borrowers, the distributions would be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in NCDL would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors looking to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NCDL is a sell at this time due to the declining NAV, poor new investment activity, and failure to support distributions. Although NCDL's portfolio maintains an efficient level of diversity across different industries, the underlying earnings are weak. The portfolio still has a low rate of non-accruals, but the growth metrics are underwhelming. Unless management allocates a greater amount of capital towards new investments, I believe that the NAV will continue to trend downward unless the dividend is cut. Therefore, I believe that the discounted price to NAV valuation is a direct reflection of the headwinds faced, rather than an attractive opportunity to accumulate.

data.title: Aehr Test Systems: AI Processing Opportunity Looks Interesting, But Don't Be In A Rush
data.description:
Introduction

Aehr Test Systems, Inc. (AEHR) is a California-based supplier of semiconductor test and burn-in equipment that is used by semi manufacturers primarily to ascertain and mark flawed chips at the wafer level before they are packed. It wouldn’t be a stretch to suggest that AEHR is something of an authority in this stress-testing space, having honed its burn-in testing expertise for over four-and-a-half decades.

It also appears that 2025 as a whole has been very rewarding for AEHR’s shareholder base; to elaborate, on a YTD basis, the AEHR stock has expanded by close to 36%, marginally outperforming other stocks from the semiconductor industry (on average) and generating almost three times the returns of its peers from the Russell 2000. Having said that, do also consider that since October the tide appears to have turned, with AEHR giving away some of its previously sizeable gains (it was up by over 80% for the first nine months of 2025).

So, should investors buy into this intermediate weakness, or can they afford to be patient?

Business Slowdown to Persist for a Couple More Quarters

Basically, investors who want to pursue AEHR should first consider that this is not a business that attracts a wide portfolio of high-volume clients, and thus, one tends to see some lumpiness across quarters; although the situation has improved from what it was a couple of years back (97% of revenue came from its top 5 clients back then), AEHR’s 5 largest clients still contribute over 75% of its revenue, and, too, cancellations and deferrals are not uncommon, given limited penalties.

This year, especially in the second half of the calendar year (although note that AEHR subscribes to a May-ending fiscal), tariff announcements have triggered some uncertainties at AEHR’s customers’ end (tariffs are also impacting the imported parts of AEHR and leaving an adverse mark on gross margins, which we’ll touch on later), and that has prevented AEHR management from providing formal guidance for the FY (year ending May 2026).

Given what we’ve written in the previous paragraph, investors should not be surprised to see that AEHR has seen quite a wobbly revenue trajectory in 2025. To elaborate, for the February-ending quarter (Q3-25), YoY revenue growth was up by an impressive 140% YoY, but in the following two quarters, revenue has slumped, with the most recent decline coming in at the -16% level.

Take note of the forward-looking backlog as well (which typically gets converted over a time frame of 12 months); sequentially, it has barely grown at around $15.5M, and on a YoY basis, it is down by -7%.

Unsurprisingly, given the tepid backlog growth, consensus suggests that AEHR revenue weakness will continue for the next couple of quarters at the very least, before growth returns in Q4-26 (the May 2026 quarter) as the currently soft Power Silicon Carbide (SiC) market (keenly linked to BEV and automotive appetite coming back, as this segment accounts for 70% of demand) is expected to start displaying some resilience by then.

Investors should also consider that the slowdown in volumes also leaves an unfavorable mark on the company’s gross margins [GM] due to underutilization of their manufacturing capacity (higher import costs and an unfavorable product mix are also playing a part in GM weakness). Note that GMs have taken quite a brutal hit over the last two quarters (down by roughly 1700bps YoY on average) and are currently at sub-40% levels.

Until volumes come back, don’t expect a meaningful uplift in margins either, although it’s fair to say they have probably bottomed out as well.

Well Positioned to Service High-Power AI Processors

Amidst these broader pressures, AEHR should not be written off because of its rising capabilities in servicing the AI processor market (the semiconductor category with the greatest growth potential through this decade), which has only been enhanced following last year’s acquisition of Incal Technology, a specialist in ultra-high-power solutions.

Offering testing and burn-in systems at the wafer level has been AEHR’s core competence, but by getting Incal on board, AEHR is now the “only company worldwide” that can also offer “packaged part” burn-in systems for high-power AI processors (besides the wafer-level expertise).

With the influx of multiple processors and memory stacks, overall power requirements go through the roof (AI GPUs can typically consume close to 1000 MW of power) and also require liquid-cooled conductive temperature control to manage the high thermal levels.

AEHR’s flagship product for this space, the Sonoma brand of burn-in systems, is not just seeing traction in new orders but also follow-on orders from hyperscalers and data center providers that are in the process of introducing new AI processors that need to be tested (collaborations are also on for future generations of processors). It’s worth noting that in the most recent quarter, the company also conducted a customer open house highlighting these Sonoma systems, with 10 different companies showing an interest in the same.

AEHR is not just resting on its laurels and has been enhancing the capabilities of Sonoma, with the newest version coming across as fully automated and increasing power per device to 2000 watts!

AEHR’s Forward Valuations Look Concerning

Last year AEHR wasn’t profitable from a GAAP perspective and ended up reporting a net loss per share of -$0.13. This year, they are expected to get back in the black, but consensus expects an EPS of just $0.06. In light of the low EPS base, AEHR’s price of $22.61 looks quite concerning, as it translates to an exorbitant multiple of 376x. To put that multiple into further context, note that it represents a 9.4x premium over other semiconductor equipment and material stocks, which can be picked up at an average forward earnings multiple of 40x.

Technicals - Loss of Momentum, With a Build-up in Shorts, Although 200dma Continues to Be Defended

When we view AEHR’s charts, we feel neutral positioning would be the right call at this juncture.

The image below highlights AEHR’s daily price movements, and what we can see is that the period of higher-highs [HHs] and higher-lows [HLs] that was evident from May to October has reversed. In November, we also saw the stock break down from its ascending trendline, and despite attempts to recoup the trendline this month, we are yet to see that transpire (reflecting a lack of bullish conviction). However, what’s encouraging to see is that AEHR has not yet given up on defending its landmark 200DMA.

Having said that, the bulls of AEHR should also be mindful of a growing cohort of short-sellers since November; after showing meaningful declines since the turn of 2025, the short-interest has grown by around 14% since November, and at 5.1M, now accounts for almost 20% of AEHR’s total float.

AEHR’s positioning vs the broad semiconductor universe should also be taken into account; earlier in Q1 of this year, AEHR may have come across as an intriguing upward mean reversion bet within this universe, as the stock’s relative strength ratio vs its peers was trading at a discount to its long-term mean of 0.057. That trade has since played out, with the current Relative Strength now perched at a 20% premium over the long-term mean.

Summary

The AEHR stock, which has experienced a commendable 2025 overall, appears to be facing some intermediate roadblocks as we close the year, driven by uncertainties at the customers’ end and underutilization of AEHR’s resources, which are also impacting margins. Management’s limited visibility and further potential top line weakness over the next couple of quarters suggest that investors don’t necessarily have to be in a mad rush to buy into this weakness, particularly when the stock is priced at an exorbitant forward earnings multiple of 376x (almost 10x more than the industry average).
